God's own character and judgment are in the dock; human behaviour is part of the evidence. His willingness to be examined in this way, to have His dealings with people become evidence for or against His character, is the posture of someone utterly confident in the truth.

The phrase 'they shall know that I am the LORD' appears over sixty times in Ezekiel, making it the most repeated refrain in the book. God acts, again and again, not simply because He has the authority to, but 'for the sake of my holy name' (Ezekiel 36:22), so that something true will be known about Him. Ezekiel 20 is a long review of Israel's history in which God explains His repeated interventions in terms of not wanting His name profaned among the nations who were watching. The watching universe matters to God because He is building a universe of free trust, not of compelled compliance, and free trust requires an accurate picture of what He is actually like.

Daniel 7:9-10 describes a courtroom vision: 'As I looked, thrones were placed, and the Ancient of Days took his seat... the court sat in judgment, and the books were opened.' A hundred million beings are present. A tyrant rules in secret; God submits His governance to the scrutiny of His creatures. Even the messenger who comes to Daniel says: 'I will tell you what is inscribed in the book of truth' (Daniel 10:21). Nothing is hidden. God does not run a secret operation and ask His people to trust Him in the dark. He opens the records and shows His people what is happening and why. This is the governing style that runs through every heavenly council scene in Scripture.

Zechariah 3 is a heavenly court scene: Joshua the high priest stands before the Angel of the Lord, with Satan at his right hand to accuse him (Zechariah 3:1). God does not exclude Satan from the court or prevent him from speaking. The accusation is heard. The response is not to ignore the record but to testify to transformation: 'See, I have taken your iniquity away from you, and I will clothe you with pure garments' (Zechariah 3:4). Joshua's record is real; the transformation is real; and both are presented openly before the watching court. God wins the argument not by suppressing the accusation but by answering it with evidence of genuine change.

The Sermon on the Mount, the parables, the healings, the table fellowship with sinners, the washing of disciples' feet, and ultimately the cross: every element of Jesus's life was a demonstration of what God is like. He did not simply announce that God loves sinners; He ate with them. He did not simply teach that the last would be first; He knelt on the floor and washed feet. 'Whoever has seen me has seen the Father' (John 14:9). The incarnation is the definitive answer to the question of what God's character is actually like: not a claim but a life, lived in full view, available to anyone who wanted to see.

honest-expression.title: God Invites Honest and Open Expression
text: God wants real communication, not performance. He already knows everything inside us completely, so keeping up a polished, acceptable front in His presence is not reverence; it is a kind of pointlessness. The only thing that disappoints Him is artificiality.

Psalm 139 makes the case for honesty from first principles: 'You know when I sit down and when I rise up; you discern my thoughts from afar... even before a word is on my tongue, you know it altogether.' Since God already sees everything, telling Him only the acceptable parts is like insisting to a doctor that you feel perfectly well while he reads your test results. The knowledge is already there; the pretence serves no one.

The Psalms are the strongest evidence of what God welcomes. They preserve, as inspired models of prayer, depression so deep the psalmist asks whether God has forgotten to be gracious (Psalm 77), the feeling of complete abandonment ('My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?', Psalm 22), and open desire for revenge ('I hate them with perfect hatred', Psalm 139; 'dashes your little ones against the rock', Psalm 137). No voice from heaven interrupts to rebuke the candour. The decisive point is this: if such honesty were wrong, the Spirit would not have inspired and preserved these prayers in Scripture, because doing so would mislead every generation that followed. They were kept because God welcomes honest self-expression rather than being offended by it.

David is described as a man after God's own heart. He was a man of blood who prayed for the ruin of his enemies. God did not endorse the content of those prayers. What He endorsed was the willingness to be genuine, because the same David who said 'I hate them with perfect hatred' could also say: 'Search me, O God, and know my heart... and see if there be any wicked way in me, and lead me in the way everlasting' (Psalm 139). God honoured the honesty that made real searching and real change possible.

Honesty before God is not, however, the end of the matter. The model prayers move from candour to surrender: from 'Lord, you know what I am feeling' to 'if I need a new heart and a right spirit, I want that.' God welcomes the raw truth so that healing can begin where we actually are, not where we pretend to be.

examples.text: The Psalms contain prayers that would shock many churchgoers. Psalm 88 ends without resolution, finishing on the word 'darkness'. Psalm 137 ends with: 'Blessed shall he be who takes your little ones and dashes them against the rock!' Psalm 139:21-22: 'Do I not hate those who hate you, O LORD?... I hate them with perfect hatred; I count them my enemies.' These were not corrected, toned down, or excluded from Scripture. They were preserved as inspired examples of prayer. The conclusion the Spirit draws is: this is acceptable before God. He is not fragile or easily offended. He knows what is inside us already, and He welcomes its honest expression rather than a sanitised performance of it.

Job's speeches become increasingly raw. He calls for a legal hearing: 'I desire to argue my case with God' (Job 13:3). He says: 'God has put me in the wrong and closed his net about me' (Job 19:6). He wants to speak directly: 'Oh, that I knew where I might find him, that I might come even to his seat!' (Job 23:3). None of this is rebuked. When God speaks from the whirlwind, He does not reprove Job for the content of his complaints; He answers him as one who deserves an answer. At the end, God's verdict is that Job has spoken rightly, while his friends, who said theologically safer things, have not. The honest questioner is treated with dignity.

The Psalms attributed to David across his life include prayers for the destruction of his enemies (Psalm 59, written when Saul sent men to kill him), laments over abandonment (Psalm 22), and transparent confessions after his worst failures (Psalm 51). God's description of David in 1 Samuel 13:14 and Acts 13:22, 'a man after my own heart', is not a claim that David was morally exemplary. David was a man of blood and failure. What God honoured was the willingness to bring everything before Him: the gratitude, the fury, the self-justification, the shame, and ultimately: 'Create in me a clean heart, O God, and renew a right spirit within me' (Psalm 51:10). That movement from honesty to surrender is the whole pattern.

not-arbitrary.title: God Is Not Arbitrary
text: God's commands make sense. His warnings describe real cause and effect rather than arbitrary penalties imposed from outside. The God of Scripture is not a sovereign who issues demands disconnected from reason; He is a person whose ways, examined closely, hold together as a trustworthy and coherent way of life.

Proverbs exists in the middle of Scripture specifically to make this point. It sits between the Law and the Prophets as a book of plain common sense, showing that genuine religion shows up in ordinary life: in how you treat your neighbour, how you handle money, what you say about someone behind their back, and how you bring up your children. God does not desire sacrifice and ceremony in the abstract; He wants people who are decent, reliable, and safe to live next to. If religious practice does not produce that kind of person, something has gone wrong.

Hosea 9:10 names a law that runs through all of Scripture: 'they became as abominable as the thing which they loved.' We become like whatever we worship and admire. Worship a crocodile, a beetle, or a frog, and the mind darkens to that level. God does not have to impose a penalty; the consequence is built into the action. This is why idolatry is so damaging: not because God adds punishment from outside, but because the corruption is inherent in the choice itself.

Even gossip, which may appear trivial, appears in Romans 1 alongside some of the worst sins described in the Bible. The reason is not prudishness; it is architectural. In the hereafter, memories will be better and sharper than they are now. If a gossip were admitted with perfect recall and a willing audience, there would be no peace for anyone. Sins that destroy trust and safety are not trivial to a God who is building a universe where people must genuinely be safe to live alongside one another.

God's warnings are invitations, not locked decrees. Joel 2:13: 'Return to the LORD your God, for He is gracious and merciful, slow to anger, and abounding in steadfast love, and relents from sending calamity.' He told Nineveh forty days, and relented when they repented. The warnings describe what is coming; they do not foreclose the possibility of turning around. A God whose warnings describe reality, and who is pleased to relent when people respond, is the opposite of arbitrary.

examples.text: Proverbs makes no appeal to divine authority; it argues from experience and consequence. 'The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom' (Proverbs 9:10), but the supporting case is built on observation: the young man who visits the adulteress loses his life like an ox going to slaughter (Proverbs 7:22-23); the diligent hand rules while the slack hand comes to poverty (Proverbs 10:4); the one who despises instruction destroys himself (Proverbs 15:32). Each warning describes what actually happens, not what God decides to impose. The book places common sense and divine instruction side by side because they point to the same reality.

'They became as abominable as the thing which they loved' (Hosea 9:10). This observation is stated as a law of the inner life: worship forms the worshipper. Israel became like the Baals they worshipped. God does not have to add punishment to idolatry; the corruption is inherent in the choice. This is what makes idolatry serious in a way that has nothing to do with offending divine dignity: it damages the worshipper's capacity to see and think and love rightly. The warning is not 'I will punish you for this' but 'this is what this does to you.' God's warnings describe what is actually happening, not what He has arbitrarily decided to impose.

Joel 2:13 restates the ancient description of God's character from Exodus 34: 'Gracious and merciful, slow to anger, and abounding in steadfast love, and relenting from disaster.' The warnings in Joel are not locked sentences handed down from an authority who cannot be moved. They describe what is coming and invite a response that will change the outcome. 'Return to the LORD your God' is the repeated call (Joel 2:12-13). When Nineveh did exactly that under Jonah's preaching, God relented immediately. A God whose warnings are conditional on human response is consistent and trustworthy in a way that a God with fixed, unalterable penalties could never be.

Micah 6:6-8 is one of the most celebrated passages in the prophets, precisely because it reduces what God requires to something morally intelligible: 'He has told you, O man, what is good; and what does the LORD require of you but to do justice, and to love kindness, and to walk humbly with your God?' The preceding verses walk through an imaginary negotiation: shall I bring thousands of rams? Shall I give my firstborn for my transgression? God's answer bypasses the escalating religious logic entirely. What He wants is justice, kindness, and humility. These are not arbitrary ritual demands; they are the shape of a life that is genuinely good to live next to.

The clean and unclean distinctions in Leviticus can look like arbitrary ritual rules until their context is read. They belonged to a comprehensive teaching system designed to make the people of God visibly different in everyday life, and to provide a consistent enacted language about access, holiness, and restoration to relationship with God. The sacrificial system as a whole was not about God needing anything; it was about providing a visible way for people who had broken the relationship to understand, step by step, what restoration involved. The system was temporary and preparatory, but it was not arbitrary. It said something true about sin, cost, and forgiveness.

delights-in-love.title: God Delights in Love
text: God is warm and personal. He is not a distant, impersonal deity presiding from some remote point in the universe. He created human love deliberately, called it very good, and placed a whole celebration of it at the centre of Scripture. This says something about the kind of person He is.

When God planned to create human beings, He could have made them any way at all. He made them male and female, and designed it so that couples would be strongly drawn to one another. The Song of Solomon exists because of this: a king who never chose most of his wives found one person he genuinely loved, and they told each other so. That this celebration of ordinary human love sits in the middle of a Bible full of law, war, and sacrifice means it matters deeply to God. He wants people to feel this way about each other. That is not the instinct of an impersonal deity.

God uses the warmest human relationships to describe His own love for His people. Through Hosea, Isaiah, Jeremiah, and the apostles, He says in effect: I feel about you the way a husband feels about his wife, the way a father feels about his children. And when even those images fall short, He reaches for something gentler still: 'As a hen gathers her chicks under her wings' (Matthew 23:37). He has tried in many and various ways to represent how warmly He feels, and making human beings capable of love and family was one of those deliberate acts.

Religion has often made people cold, distant, and severe, treating human love itself as suspect. Both the cheapening of love into careless excess and the condemning of it as sinful are perversions of the same good gift. God placed the Song of Solomon in Scripture as a remedy against both. A God who delights in love is not a God to approach in dread, but One whose character is the very basis on which 1 John 4:18 stands: 'Perfect love casts out fear.'

examples.text: The Song of Solomon is eight chapters of poetry celebrating human love between a man and a woman: their attraction, their longing, their delight in each other's presence. It is warm and explicit. It belongs in the middle of Scripture, between Ecclesiastes and Isaiah, surrounded by wisdom literature on one side and the great prophets on the other. Its placement is not accidental. God designed human beings to experience this kind of love and included its celebration in Scripture deliberately. A God who puts these feelings into human beings and then celebrates them in writing is not a remote deity indifferent to the ordinary texture of human life.

God's choice to tell Hosea's story in terms of a marriage illustrates how God wants to be understood. He could have used legal language, or contractual language, or language of national treaty. Instead He chose the most intimate human relationship available. 'I will betroth you to me forever. I will betroth you to me in righteousness and in justice, in steadfast love and in mercy. I will betroth you to me in faithfulness. And you shall know the LORD' (Hosea 2:19-20). The repetition of 'I will betroth you' three times, each adding more attributes of the relationship, is poetry trying to say something too large for a single statement. God feels about His people the way a husband feels about a wife he is genuinely in love with.

Ecclesiastes is often read as a book of world-weary scepticism, but at several points it opens into something warmer. 'Go, eat your bread with joy, and drink your wine with a merry heart, for God has already approved what you do... Enjoy life with the wife whom you love, all the days of your vain life that he has given you under the sun' (Ecclesiastes 9:7-9). The context is the recognition that life is uncertain and short, but the response is not despair; it is the enjoyment of the good things God has given. God's approval is already there. The ordinary pleasures of shared meals, celebration, and loving companionship are gifts from a God who delights in His children enjoying what He has made.

Jesus's description of what He wanted to do with Jerusalem captures the warmth of God's feeling with an image from the natural world: 'O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, the city that kills the prophets and stones those who are sent to it! How often would I have gathered your children together as a hen gathers her brood under her wings, and you were not willing!' (Matthew 23:37). The image is not of a king, or a judge, or even a father. It is a hen, a creature that shelters her vulnerable young not through power but through warmth and closeness. Jesus chose that picture deliberately. The God revealed in Jesus gathers rather than repels, shelters rather than exposes, and grieved not at the thought of judgment but at the thought of those He could not reach.
